metadata.title: The GIGO Gate: Doubt Your Data Before It Moves Anything
metadata.playlist: Build It, Then Doubt It — The Skeptical Reallocation Tool
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Computational Skepticism for AI — Reallocation Engine (100 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 3 of 9. GIGO stands for garbage in, garbage out. A reallocation engine that acts on corrupted data does not just produce a wrong answer — it confidently moves the wrong resource. This episode builds the gate: a set of explicit checks the data must pass before the engine is allowed to act on it. Not 'the data looks fine.' A checkable standard with a rejection log that honestly documents what fails.

props.segment: The GIGO Gate: Doubt Your Data Before It Moves Anything
props.command: Skeptical EDA (what does this data assume?) → checkable quality standard → rejection log → the hidden failure the gate cannot catch.
props.runningText: GIGO = garbage in, resources reallocated wrong — gate the data before the engine acts on it…
output: GIGO gate function: accepted records + rejection log (record_id, gate_failed, disposition)
Named hidden failure: the record the gate accepts that you most want to doubt

beats.act: skeptical EDA — what does this data assume?
beats.narration_text: Skeptical exploratory data analysis. Regular EDA asks: what does this data contain? Skeptical EDA asks: what does this data assume that is not true? The difference is the direction of questioning. Standard EDA describes the distribution and looks for outliers. Skeptical EDA asks what protocol produced this dataset, whether that protocol changed mid-collection, whether the definition of the key variable was stable over time, and whether the absence of a value means something different from zero. For a reallocation engine, the most dangerous assumption is that the historical data reflects the world you are about to act on. If the dataset records past reallocations, and past reallocations were themselves biased, the engine is learning to replicate a bias, not discover an optimal allocation. That failure is invisible to descriptive statistics. It requires asking: who collected this data, for what purpose, and what did they fail to record?

props.artifactHeading: Not 'what does it contain' — 'what does it assume that isn't true?'
artifactLines: Regular EDA: what's in the data? Skeptical EDA: what does the data assume?
Questions: What protocol produced it? Did it change mid-collection?
Is the key variable definition stable over time? Does 'absent' = 'zero'?
Danger: historical allocations + historical bias → engine learns to replicate the bias.
That failure is invisible to descriptive stats. Ask: who collected this, for what, and what's missing?
props.sparkLine: The most dangerous assumption: that the historical data reflects the world you're about to act on.

beats.narration_text: The quality gate. A quality gate is a checkable standard a human can verify — not 'rows with missing values,' that is a computation. A checkable standard names the source, the timestamp, the required non-null fields, and the protocol condition. 'A valid record has a timestamp within the collection window, a non-null allocation value, and was recorded by the same measurement protocol used for records after May 2023.' That standard can be checked by a person who does not run the code. Implement that standard in the GIGO gate function. When a record fails the gate, log it: the record identifier, the gate it failed, and the disposition — excluded or flagged for manual review. The rejection log is a graded deliverable. What you log honestly about the data you could not use is more valuable than a clean table that hides the failures. The question the grader is asking: did you find the failure the data was designed or happened to hide?

props.artifactHeading: Checkable by a human — not just a computation
artifactLines: Quality standard: names source + timestamp + required non-null fields + protocol condition.
'Missing values' is a computation. 'Same protocol used after May 2023' is a checkable standard.
Gate function: (dataframe) → (accepted_df, rejection_log_df with record_id + gate_failed + disposition).
Dispositions: excluded OR flagged for manual review.
Rejection log = graded. Honest documentation of failures > clean table that hides them.
props.sparkLine: What you honestly log about the data you couldn't use earns more marks than a spotless table.

beats.act: the hidden failure — what the gate cannot catch
beats.narration_text: Find the failure the data was built or happened to hide. Every real dataset has a structural failure: a variable whose absence means something, a collection gap during a period when the resource constraint was unusually high or low, a class of records that was systematically underreported because of who was doing the reporting. Your GIGO gate catches the explicit failures — missing values, out-of-range fields, protocol drift. The skeptical pass is the one after that: look at the records the gate accepted and ask whether the gate is right to accept them. A record that passes every gate check can still encode a bias if the protocol that produced it was biased. Name one case where the gate passes a record that you have reason to doubt, and document why you doubt it. That finding — not the gate itself but the honest analysis of what the gate cannot catch — is what earns the marks for this component.

props.artifactHeading: Find what the gate passes that you have reason to doubt
artifactLines: Every dataset: a variable whose absence means something, a collection gap, a reporting bias.
Gate catches: missing values, out-of-range, protocol drift.
Skeptical pass: look at gate-accepted records — is the gate right to accept them?
A record can pass every check and still encode bias from a biased collection protocol.
Name one: 'the gate passes this record, but I doubt it because [reason].' That's the finding.
props.sparkLine: The finding isn't the gate — it's the honest analysis of what the gate cannot catch.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the GIGO Gate. Skeptical EDA asks what the data assumes, not just what it contains. The quality standard is checkable by a person: timestamp, source, non-null fields, protocol condition. The rejection log names the record, the gate it failed, and its disposition. Find the hidden failure — the record that passes all the checks but still carries a structural problem the gate was not designed to catch. What you honestly document about the data's failure earns more marks than a clean table. Episode 4 traces where that failure enters the output.

props.artifactTitle: The GIGO Gate: Doubt Your Data Before It Moves Anything
artifactLines: Skeptical EDA: 'what does this data assume?' not 'what does it contain?'
Quality standard: checkable by a human — source + timestamp + non-null + protocol.
Gate function: accepted_df + rejection_log (record_id + gate_failed + disposition).
Find the hidden failure: one record the gate accepts that you have reason to doubt.
Honest rejection log > clean table. Ep 4: trace where the failure enters the output.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The GIGO gate prompt. Paste this into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I am building a GIGO gate for my reallocation engine. My dataset is: [describe — columns, rows, source, collection protocol]. Here are the key failure modes I believe are in this dataset: [list two or three — missing values, protocol changes, biased collection, underreported classes, etc.]. Draft three things. First: a quality standard in plain language — three to five checkable conditions that a valid record must meet, stated as a human-readable rule, not as code. Second: a Python function signature for the GIGO gate — takes a DataFrame, returns a tuple of accepted DataFrame and rejection log DataFrame with columns: record underscore ID, gate underscore failed, disposition. Third: one sentence naming the failure the gate cannot catch — the record that would pass every check but still carries a structural problem in how it was collected or what it fails to represent. After you get the draft, find the record the gate accepts that you most want to doubt. The one that passed all the checks and still makes you uncomfortable. That discomfort is the finding. Document it honestly — it is worth more than a gate that catches only what you designed it to catch.

props.segment: The GIGO Gate: Doubt Your Data Before It Moves Anything
props.command: Dataset: [cols/rows/source/protocol]. Failure modes: [2–3]. → Quality standard (3–5 human-readable rules) + gate function signature (accepted_df, rejection_log with record_id/gate_failed/disposition) + one sentence on what the gate cannot catch.
props.runningText: the record the gate accepts that makes you uncomfortable — that discomfort is the finding…

beats.narration_text: The GIGO Gate: Doubt Your Data Before It Moves Anything. Liam, in for Bear.
